examples.title: LSTM Forward Pass Implementation
examples.description: Implementation of forward pass in LSTM with all gates
examples.code:
import numpy as np

def sigmoid(x):
    return 1 / (1 + np.exp(-x))

def tanh(x):
    return np.tanh(x)

def lstm_forward(x, h_prev, C_prev, Wy, Wyf, Wyi, Wyo, Wc, by, bfo, bio, bo, bc):
    # Concatenate previous hidden state and current input
    concat = np.concatenate([h_prev, x])
    
    # Forget gate: decide what to discard
    f_t = sigmoid(np.dot(Wfo, concat) + bfo)
    
    # Input gate: decide what new information to add
    i_t = sigmoid(np.dot(Wyi, concat) + bio)
    C_tilde = tanh(np.dot(Wc, concat) + bc)
    
    # Update cell state
    C_t = f_t * C_prev + i_t * C_tilde
    
    # Output gate: decide what to output
    o_t = sigmoid(np.dot(Wyo, concat) + bo)
    h_t = o_t * tanh(C_t)
    
    return h_t, C_t
examples.explanation: This code implements the forward pass of LSTM showing how forget gate controls what to discard, input gate controls what new information to add, and output gate controls what to reveal. The cell state acts as memory highway.
examples.timeComplexity: O(d × d + d × n) per time step
examples.spaceComplexity: O(d) for states, O(d²) for weights

examples.title: LSTM Training with BPTT
examples.description: Pseudocode showing backpropagation through time for LSTM
examples.code:
ALGORITHM LSTM_BPTT
BEGIN
  INPUT: sequence of T time steps, initial h0, C0
  
  # Forward Pass - Store all intermediate values
  FOR t = 1 to T DO
    concat_t = [h_{t-1}, x_t]
    
    f_t = σ(W_f × concat_t + b_f)    # Forget gate
    i_t = σ(W_i × concat_t + b_i)    # Input gate
    C̃_t = tanh(W_C × concat_t + b_C) # Candidate
    
    C_t = f_t × C_{t-1} + i_t × C̃_t   # Cell state update
    
    o_t = σ(W_o × concat_t + b_o)    # Output gate
    h_t = o_t × tanh(C_t)            # Hidden state
    
    STORE f_t, i_t, C̃_t, C_t, o_t, h_t
  END FOR
  
  # Backward Pass - Compute gradients
  Initialize gradients: dC_next = 0, dh_next = 0
  
  FOR t = T down to 1 DO
    # Output gradient
    dC_t += dh_next × tanh(C_t)  # from h_t
    do_t = dC_t × tanh(C_t) × σ'(o_t)
    
    # Cell state gradient flows unchanged
    dC_next = dC_t × f_t
    df_t = dC_t × C_{t-1}
    di_t = dC_t × C̃_t
    dC̃_t = dC_t × i_t
    
    # Compute weight gradients and accumulate
    dW_f += df_t × concat_t
    dW_i += di_t × concat_t
    dW_o += do_t × concat_t
    dW_C += dC̃_t × concat_t
    
    dh_next = (W_o^T × do_t) + (W_f^T × df_t) + (W_i^T × di_t) + (W_C^T × dC̃_t)
  END FOR
  
  # Update weights using gradients
  RETURN updated_weights
END
examples.explanation: BPTT unrolls the LSTM through time and computes gradients backwards. Key insight: gradients flow through cell state without vanishing because of the additive update (multiplication by forget gate, not weight matrix).
examples.timeComplexity: O(T × d²) where T is sequence length, d is hidden dimension
examples.spaceComplexity: O(T × d) to store intermediate values for all time steps

examples.title: Bidirectional LSTM for Sequence Labeling
examples.description: Using Keras to implement bidirectional LSTM for sequence labeling tasks
examples.code:
from tensorflow.keras.models import Sequential
from tensorflow.keras.layers import Bidirectional, LSTM, Dense, TimeDistributed

# Define Bidirectional LSTM model for sequence labeling
model = Sequential([
    # Embedding layer for input words
    Embedding(input_dim=10000, output_dim=128, input_length=20),
    
    # Bidirectional LSTM processes sequence in both directions
    Bidirectional(
        LSTM(64, return_sequences=True),
        merge_mode='concat'
    ),
    
    # Another Bidirectional LSTM layer for deeper representation
    Bidirectional(
        LSTM(32, return_sequences=True),
        merge_mode='concat'
    ),
    
    # TimeDistributed dense layer for each time step output
    TimeDistributed(Dense(num_tags, activation='softmax'))
])

model.compile(
    optimizer='adam',
    loss='categorical_crossentropy',
    metrics=['accuracy']
)

# Train the model
model.fit(X_train, y_train, epochs=10, batch_size=32)

# Predict - uses both past and future context
predictions = model.predict(X_test)
examples.explanation: Bidirectional LSTM runs two LSTMs: one forward (left-to-right) and one backward (right-to-left). For each position, it gets context from both directions, crucial for tasks like named entity recognition where future words provide important information.
examples.timeComplexity: O(2 × T × d²) - roughly double standard LSTM
examples.spaceComplexity: O(2 × d) for storing forward and backward states
